coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and accurate fluorescent genosensor for Staphylococcus aureus detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Rui
Haruna, Suleiman A.
Ali, Shujat
Xu, Jing
Zhang, Yunlian
Lü, Peng
Li, Huanhuan
Chen, Quansheng
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic bacteria, as a major cause of foodborne diseases, pose a serious threat to human health, which urges the development of sensitive and accurate detection methods. Herein, a fluorescent genosensor was designed for Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) detection based on fluorescent probes and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). The π-π stacking and hydrogen bond interactions between GOQDs and specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) of the fluorescent probes were exploited by the developed genosensor. The interactions brought the GOQDs to the surface of the fluorescent probes, quenching strong upconversion fluorescence. However, the hybridization of the recognition probes anchored on the fluorescent reporters with complementary nuc target obstructed the interactions, causing the fluorescence to gradually increase with the increasing concentration of complementary nuc target. Consequently, the target-specific sensor exhibited high sensitivity with a LOD of 0.98 × 10−17 mol L−1 and a linear range from 1 × 10−17 to 1 × 10−11 mol L−1. Moreover, the genosensor was successfully applied for real sample analysis with excellent recoveries. Significantly, the sensor could be used for the detection of other pathogens in actual samples by replacing recognition probes and primers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconverting nanoparticles
Graphene oxide quantum dots
Fluorescent genosensor
Staphylococcus aureus

PCR amplification and agarose gel electrophoresis
S. aureus detection by the fluorescent genosensor
S. aureus Detection by real-time PCR
Detection principle of the fluorescent genosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of UCNPs-GOQDs genosensor for S. aureus detection.

Characterization of UCNPs. (A) TEM image of the OA-UCNPs, (B) TEM image of the COOH-UCNPs, (C) XRD pattern of UCNPs, (D) EDS spectrum of UCNPs, (E) FT-IR spectroscopy of UCNPs, (F) Zeta potential of carboxyl modified UCNPs, (G) UV–vis absorption spectra of UCNPs and fluorescent probes, and (H) the fluorescence spectra of UCNPs and fluorescent probes. (n = 3).

Optimization of experimental parameters of the fluorescent genosensor. (A) Optimization of GOQDs amount. Effects of the incubation time (B) and the incubation temperature (C) on the detection system. The error bars were derived from the SD of three measurements.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the genosensor in the presence of different concentrations of S. aureus nuc. (B) Calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity for nuc concentrations ranging from 1 × 10−17 to 1 × 10−11 mol L−1. The error bars were derived from the SD of three measurements.

The specificity assessment of the designed genosensor. (A) Fluorescence responses of the genosensor to different types of oligos. (B) The comparison of fluorescence responses to the complementary oligos (1), completely non-complementary oligos (2), single-base mismatch oligos (3) and blank control (4). The error bars were derived from the SD of three measurements.

Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis (1.5%) of PCR amplicon. (A) Bacterial genomes were respectively extracted from pure pathogens cultured in LB. Lanes: M, DL5000 DNA Marker; 1, the positive test sample S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™); 2–5, negative control samples containing B. cereus (CICC 10041), S. typhimurium (CICC 22956), E. coli O157:H7 (CICC 21530), and ddH2O. (B) The gDNA was extracted from S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™) added into milk. Lanes: M, DL5000 DNA Marker; 1, blank control sample ddH2O; 2, 3, negative control samples without S. aureus in milk; 4, the positive sample S. aureus.
Recovery results of the genosensor for the detection of S. aureus ATCC® 29213™ and S. aureus ATCC® 6538™ (n = 8).

row.entry: The detection of S. aureus ATCC® 29213™
The detection of S. aureus ATCC® 6538™

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reproducibility of the developed method for the detection of S. aureus ATCC® 29213™ in milk and normal human serum (n = 8).

head.ce:title: A sensitive and accurate fluorescent genosensor for Staphylococcus aureus detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic bacteria, as a major cause of foodborne diseases, pose a serious threat to human health, which urges the development of sensitive and accurate detection methods. Herein, a fluorescent genosensor was designed for Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) detection based on fluorescent probes and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). The π-π stacking and hydrogen bond interactions between GOQDs and specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) of the fluorescent probes were exploited by the developed genosensor. The interactions brought the GOQDs to the surface of the fluorescent probes, quenching strong upconversion fluorescence. However, the hybridization of the recognition probes anchored on the fluorescent reporters with complementary nuc target obstructed the interactions, causing the fluorescence to gradually increase with the increasing concentration of complementary nuc target. Consequently, the target-specific sensor exhibited high sensitivity with a LOD of 0.98 × 10−17 mol L−1 and a linear range from 1 × 10−17 to 1 × 10−11 mol L−1. Moreover, the genosensor was successfully applied for real sample analysis with excellent recoveries. Significantly, the sensor could be used for the detection of other pathogens in actual samples by replacing recognition probes and primers.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last few years, foodborne infections have skyrocketed owing primarily to the consumption of contaminated foods containing harmful foodborne pathogens [1]. Among these pathogens, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a typical gram-positive, widely distributed, and highly virulent foodborne pathogen that causes illnesses such as food poisoning, skin infections, endocarditis, and sepsis [2–5]. To overcome these difficulties, a sensitive and accurate approach is required for the detection of S. aureus.

Currently, several detection techniques for S. aureus have been reported. The traditional culture-based approaches include enrichment culture, selective plate separation, and biochemical identification. Despite their simplicity and stability, these procedures are time-consuming and labor-intensive, making them unsuitable for public health emergencies and food poisoning outbreaks [6–8]. Alternatively, molecular biology methods such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and immunological assays were employed as culture-based method replacements [9–11]. Regardless of its sensitivity and speed, the PCR process is costly and needs experienced operators, limiting its widespread deployment [12]. Similarly, the immunological techniques mostly use antibodies to precisely capture pathogens. However, they are constantly plagued by bioactivity maintenance and low stability [13]. To meet the current detection needs, various novel analytical assays have been applied for the detection of S. aureus, such as colorimetric methods [14,15], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [7,16], electrochemical methods [17,18], and flow cytometry-based assays [19,20]. In particular, the fluorescent nanomaterial-based method has been developed as a popular and successful tool for foodborne pathogens detection. This approach has piqued the interest of researchers due to its exceptional characteristics such as ease of operation, quick reaction, and great sensitivity. Previously, quantum dots and metal nanoclusters have been used for the detection of bacterial infections. Unfortunately, for real samples analysis, low water solubility or potential cytotoxicity of these fluorescent nanomaterials, and susceptibility to interference from the detection environment limit their further application [21,22]. Therefore, a reliable, safe, and stable method is desperately needed for S. aureus detection.

Alternatively, photon upconversion, the process of combining two or more low-energy photons into one higher-energy photon [23], is at the heart of advanced optical sensing technologies. Lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are an attractive fluorescent nanomaterial, because they exhibit outstanding properties such as remarkable anti-Stokes photoluminescence, high quantum yields, long fluorescence lifetime, excellent signal-to-noise ratio, and low toxicity [24,25]. Fluorescent sensors based on UCNPs have been applied for S. aureus detection, in which the specific antibody was used as a recognition probe [26]. However, external factors such as temperature and pH, are prone to influencing antibody activity, resulting in a high requirement for storage conditions.

Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), as a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, show tremendous potential in sensing applications due to their advantageous features as graphene oxide (GO) sheets, such as high specific surface area, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, superior electrical conductivity, high mobility, and good water solubility [27,28]. More importantly, compared with GO sheets, GOQDs have smaller sizes, so single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) can be adsorbed on the nanomaterial surface more spontaneously by π-π stacking interaction between nucleobases and sp2 carbon atoms [21]. In addition, it has been reported that GOQDs could effectively quench the fluorescence from 6-FAM-ssDNA, JOE-ssDNA, and Cy3-ssDNA mainly due to π-π stacking interactions [29]. The emission wavelengths of these fluorescent probes are 520, 548, and 570 nm respectively, which are close to the corresponding wavelength of the characteristic peak of green UCNPs. Furthermore, the -OH on the surface of GOQDs can form hydrogen bonds with the fluorescent probe's base, accelerating fluorescent probe adsorption. To date, the biosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between UCNPs-ssDNA and GOQDs has not yet been properly explored for S. aureus detection.

Herein, a sensitive and accurate fluorescent genosensor based on FRET was developed for S. aureus detection. In the strategy, the genosensor exploited π-π stacking and hydrogen bond interactions between GOQDs and specific ssDNA on the UCNPs to stimulate GOQDs to assemble the surface of fluorescent probes, which caused upconversion fluorescence quenching. However, in the presence of the S. aureus specific gene, namely the species-specific gene, the thermonuclear gene nuc [30], the double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) formation (due to base pair specific interactions) hindered the assembly of GOQDs on the fluorescent probe surface, resulting in fluorescence enhancement. Using this novel strategy, the fluorescent genosensor was established for S. aureus nuc detection. Furthermore, it has great potential to be applied for the detection of other pathogens in actual samples by substituting corresponding recognition probes and primers.

Rare earth chlorides including YbCl3.6 H2O, ErCl3.6 H2O, YCl3.6 H2O, and 1-octadecene, oleic acid, 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)−1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), poly (acrylic acid sodium salt), and agarose were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ammonium fluoride (NH4F), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), chloroform, methanol, and toluene were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Energy Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) was obtained from Nanjing XF Nano Material Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Bacterial genomic DNA (gDNA) extraction kit and QIAamp DNA Blood Mini Kit were purchased from Tiangen Biotechnology (Beijing, China) and QIAGEN Inc. (Hilden, Germany), respectively. KOD-Plus-Neo from TOYOBO (Shanghai) Biotech CO., Ltd was used for polymerase chain reaction (PCR). TB Green® Premix Ex Taq™ II (Tli RNaseH Plus) was purchased from Takara Biotechnology (Dalian, China) for real-time PCR [31]. In this study, the primers and recognize probes were designed by Primer 5 software and Primer-BLAST of National Center for Biotechnology Information, and all ssDNA and primers were prepared by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) for the detection of nuc gene in S. aureus and presented in Table S1. In addition, mass spectrometry analysis of the recognition probe and target nuc ssDNA was exhibited in Fig. S1, indicating the correctness of the synthesized sequences. Double-distilled water (ddH2O) was prepared and used throughout the experiment.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM), JEOL JEM-2100 was used for visualization of UCNPs and GOQDs. The crystallinity of UCNPs was characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker AXS, Ltd.). The vibrational spectra were collected on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Nicolet Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Co., U.S.A.) using the potassium bromide method. The absorption spectra were measured by a spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiment was performed on a Thermo Theta Probe spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The ζ-potential measurements were carried out using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., U.K.). The spectrum of energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was obtained by an EDS apparatus (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). Upconversion fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrum detection system (Zolix instruments Co., Ltd. China) at 980 nm laser light [32].

To keep the experimenters secure, a biological safety cabinet (Telstar, Spain) was employed. The gDNA quality was assessed and evaluated using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Waltham, MA, USA). T100™ thermal cycler (Bio-Rad, USA) was used to amplify the target gene using specific primers. For agarose gel electrophoresis and image analysis, the Liuyi electrophoresis system (Beijing, China) and Tanon 5200 image analysis system (Shanghai, China) were utilized, respectively. Real-time PCR was carried out using QuantStudio™ 3 and 5 real-time PCR systems (Applied Biosystems, USA).

Firstly, a modified high-temperature thermal decomposition approach was used to prepare hydrophobic UCNPs [33]. Briefly, a methanol-dissolved combination of ErCl3.6 H2O (0.03 mmol), YbCl3.6 H2O (0.17 mmol), and YCl3.6 H2O (0.8 mmol) was transferred to a three-necked glass flask. Then, 7 mL 1-octadecene and 3 mL oleic acid were added to the above solution [34]. Afterward, the mixture was heated to eliminate water using magnetic stirring and argon flow, and then cooled to 25 °C. Following that, a 10 mL methanol solution containing 4 mmol NH4F and 2.5 mmol NaOH was dripped into the mixture and rapidly agitated. Following full methanol evaporation, the mixture was gradually heated to 300 °C for 1 h, and then cooled to ambient temperature. The products were centrifuged and washed multiple times with ethanol and cyclohexane (v: v= 1:1). Finally, oleic acid-capped UCNPs (OA-UCNPs) were dried overnight at 60 °C.

Next, the surface of UCNPs was modified via a ligand exchange method to obtain water-soluble UCNPs. For this purpose, 30 mg of OA-UCNPs were dispersed in a solution containing 2 mL of chloroform and 3 mL of toluene. Then, 8 mL poly (acrylic acid sodium salt) solution (25 mg mL−1) was prepared and added into the above mixture and stirred overnight. The products were collected by centrifugation (11,000 rpm, 10 min), and then washed several times with 50% ethanol. The obtained hydrophilic COOH-UCNPs were redispersed in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4).

The previously reported procedure with some modifications was used for the synthesis of amino-ssDNA conjugated COOH-UCNPs [35,36]. To activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of the UCNPs, 15 mg EDC and 15 mg NHS were mixed with 5 mL COOH-UCNPs (1 mg mL−1) for 2 h at 4 °C. The mixture was centrifuged, washed multiple times with ddH2O, and then redispersed in 5 mL HEPES buffer (10 mM). Subsequently, 263 μL of NH2-ssDNA (100 μM) was added into the mixture and stirred for 10 h at 4 °C. The precipitations were rinsed with ddH2O after centrifugation (11,000 rpm, 10 min), and the obtained fluorescent probes were dispersed in 5 mL HEPES buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation and gDNA Extraction of S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™) was chosen as the target bacteria for detection in this work, with E. coli O157:H7 (CICC 21530), B. cereus (CICC 10041), and S. typhimurium (CICC 22956) stored in our laboratory serving as negative controls. To gain further insight into the feasibility of the developed method, S. aureus (ATCC® 6538™) was used and detected. Firstly, all strains were streaked on Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plates and then cultivated overnight at 37 °C. For each strain, an isolated colony was inoculated in LB liquid medium to cultivate overnight at 37 °C at 180 rpm. Corresponding bacterial cultures were centrifuged, and then used to extract the corresponding gDNA using a bacterial gDNA extraction kit. The spectrophotometer was used to determine the concentration and purity of four different types of gDNA obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCR amplification and agarose gel electrophoresis
ce:section.ce:para: The PCR reaction mixture contained 5 μL of 10 ×PCR Buffer, 1.5 μL of each of the forward and reverse primers (0.3 μM), 5 μL of dNTPs (2 mM), 3 μL of MgSO4 (25 mM), 1 μL of KOD-Plus-Neo (1 U/μL), 2 μL of gDNA and 31 μL of ddH2O. The amplification was performed in a T100™ thermal cycler (Bio-Rad, USA). In detail, pre-denaturation was carried out at 94 °C for 2 min, followed by 30 cycles of denaturation at 98 °C for 10 s, annealing at 55 °C for 30 s for S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli O157:H7, and S. typhimurium, respectively, and extension at 68 °C for 20 s. Finally, the extension was completed at 68 °C for 7 min, after which the products were maintained at 4 °C. To monitor any possible contamination, the positive control, and negative controls (PCR without gDNA of S. aureus) were treated in parallel with each batch of samples [37]. Afterward, the corresponding amplification products were mixed with 10 ×gelred and 6 ×loading buffer before being examined by 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis. The photos were captured using a Tanon 5200 image analysis system. Moreover, a PCR product puriﬁcation kit was used to purify the amplification products of the S. aureus nuc gene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. aureus detection by the fluorescent genosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical detection procedure, 200 μL of varying concentrations of target gene nuc (ranging from 10−17 mol L−1 to 10−11 mol L−1) in the hybridization buffer (20 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 0.3 M NaCl, pH 8.24) were mixed with each 200 μL solution of the fluorescent probes (0.5 mg mL−1), and then incubated at 94 °C for 4 min, respectively. Subsequently, the temperature of the samples was gradually reduced to 40 °C, and then kept at 40 °C for 30 min to completely execute the hybridization procedure. Next, the hybridization products were collected by centrifugation (9500 rpm, 8 min), washed multiple times, and redispersed in 400 μL hybridization buffer. To the mixture above, 600 μL of GOQDs (1 mg mL−1) was added and incubated for 1 h. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by the fluorescence spectrum detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. aureus Detection by real-time PCR
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the developed fluorescent genosensor with other assays, real-time PCR was analyzed. We prepared ten-fold serial dilutions of the gDNA extracted from S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™). In this case, the target gene was the crucial pathogenic gene nuc in S. aureus. Standard real-time PCR was performed in a 20 μL reaction system including 10 μL TB Green® Premix Ex Taq™ II, 0.4 μL ROX Reference Dye Ⅱ, 0.8 μL forward primer, 0.8 μL reverse primer, 7 μL ddH2O and 1 μL varying amounts of input gDNA [32]. The reaction was run on the real-time PCR system. The procedure was as follows, stage 1: predenature by 1 cycle at 95 °C for 5 min; stage 2: PCR reaction followed by 40 cycles at 95 °C for 10 s and then 55 °C for 30 s; stage 3: addition of melting curve by 1 cycle at 95 °C for 1 min, 55 °C for 30 s and then 95 °C for 30 s

To further evaluate the performance of S. aureus detection, the developed fluorescent genosensor was applied in real sample detection. To begin, the pasteurized milk obtained from a local supermarket was filtered through a sterile membrane to eliminate the contaminant flora. The normal human serum without S. aureus was obtained from Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories, Inc. (West Grove, USA). Next, milk and the normal human serum without S. aureus were respectively inoculated with S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™), and the bacteria were concentrated by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 3 min, followed by bacterial gDNA extraction, which was utilized for PCR amplification to obtain the targets. Thereafter, the concentration of the targets was measured, diluted, and used as the test’s background concentration. In the detection system, the standard target nuc with concentrations of 0.20 × 10−13, 0.40 × 10−13, 0.60 × 10−13, and 0.80 × 10−13 mol L−1 were introduced, accordingly. Finally, the developed fluorescent genosensor was used to detect these samples, and the recoveries were calculated. Additionally, S. aureus (ATCC® 6538™) was added to milk and detected using the method described above to further validate the feasibility of the developed method.

All of the tests were carried out a minimum of three times. The data, which were generated in random order from treatments and duplicates, were analyzed using Origin, GraphPad Prism, and Microsoft Excel. The fluorescence intensity was shown as a mean ± standard deviation (SD). The mean difference was evaluated by the two-tailed t-test if it was necessary. Statistically, P < 0.05 denotes a significant difference, whereas P > 0.05 implies an insignificant difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of the fluorescent genosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fundamental principle of the fabricated fluorescent genosensor for S. aureus detection is specifically presented in Fig. 1. The genosensor was made up of three parts: UCNPs was served as the fluorescent reporters, GOQDs were used as the nanoquenchers, and the designed nuc ssDNA was employed as the recognition probe. Specific nuc ssDNA was conjugated onto the surface of UCNPs via a coupling procedure between the carboxyl and the amino groups to obtain UCNPs-ssDNA as fluorescent probes. At an excitation wavelength of 980 nm, UCNPs-labeled probes displayed strong upconversion fluorescence [38]. The π-π stacking and hydrogen bond interactions between GOQDs and fluorescent probes’ ssDNA were used in the developed genosensor. The interactions could easily bring the GOQDs to the surface of the UCNPs-ssDNA, reducing the distance between the fluorescent probes and the nanoquenchers to less than 10 nm (The ssDNA on the surface of UCNPs and their complementary nuc target could form dsDNA (20 bp), the length of the dsDNA was calculated to be about 6.8 nm, so the distance of the UCNPs-ssDNA and GOQDs was estimated to be less than 10 nm). Furthermore, FRET occurred as a result of spectral overlap between the UCNPs fluorescence emission and GOQDs absorption, which effectively quenched the fluorescent probes’ high upconversion fluorescence [21]. However, in the presence of the complementary nuc target, the hybridization of the recognition probes anchored on the fluorescent reporters with these targets obstructed the interaction capacity of the fluorescent probes and the GOQDs, resulting in a phenomenon that caused the GOQDs and the fluorescent probes to separate. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity increased steadily as the concentration of complementary nuc target increased, and the fluorescence intensity directly reflected the nuc target concentration. Furthermore, the designed specific recognition probes have a great superiority in mismatched discrimination.

A sequence of characterizations was performed on UCNPs to determine their shape, size, crystal phase, composition analysis, and surface modification. TEM micrographs revealed that the prepared OA-UCNPs had good dispersibility and were uniform in size, with an approximate diameter of 50 nm ( Fig. 2A). The oleic acid ligand of the UCNPs surface made them hydrophobic. To further apply the UCNPs as fluorescent reporters, the hydrophobic UCNPs were modified and converted to hydrophilic by the ligand exchange method. TEM image (Fig. 2B) exhibited that the obtained COOH-UCNPs were effectively dispersed in water and their morphology and size were found to be stable. The carboxyl groups of the UCNPs surface made them hydrophilic. XRD patterns (Fig. 2C) indicated that the prepared UCNPs nanocrystals were a pure hexagonal phase, with the locations and intensities of diffraction peaks compatible with the hexagonal β-NaYF4 phase (JCPDS Standard Card No. 28–1192) and no additional impurities found [38]. NaYF4 is commonly selected and used as a host material for UCNPs, owing to high chemical stability and low phonon energies by the fluorides. Due to its ladder-like energy levels, Er3+ is commonly utilized as an activator. To boost upconversion fluorescence efficiency, a sensitizer, notably Yb3+, is frequently co-doped with Er3+ to take advantage of the efficient energy transfer upconversion process between them [24]. The EDS spectrum exhibited that the synthesized substrate contained Na, Y, F, Yb, and Er elements (Fig. 2D). In addition, the surface properties of UCNPs were also studied using FT-IR spectroscopy. For OA-UCNPs, as depicted in Fig. 2E, a broad absorption band at around 3402 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of -OH. Two strong bands, centered at 2927 and 2854 cm−1, were found, which could be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -CH2- in the long alkyl chain, respectively. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the nanoparticles before the ligand exchange were related to the transmission bands at 1558 and 1450 cm−1. Following the ligand exchange, a new peak at 1720 cm−1, assigned to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl groups [39], appeared accompanied by the decline of absorption peaks at 2927, 2854, 1558, and 1450 cm−1, indicating the existence of more carboxyl groups on the nanoparticle surface [40]. Based on the above FT-IR characterization, it was concluded that carboxyl groups were successfully bonded to the UCNPs surface. The ζ-potential measurement showed that the COOH-UCNPs were negatively charged, with a Zeta potential of − 21.4 mV, providing them with exceptional water stability (Fig. 2F). Following the nuc ssDNA accession, a new high absorption peak at 260 nm appeared in the absorption spectrum of UCNPs (Fig. 2G). The results suggested that the fluorescent probes were successfully prepared following the literature [32]. After excitation with a 980 nm laser, the fluorescence properties of UCNPs and fluorescent probes at the same concentration were also investigated. As denoted in Fig. 2H, the target nuc ssDNA conjunction procedure had no discernible effect on the fluorescence intensity of nanoparticles. These findings suggested that the UCNPs could be used as fluorescent signal reporters.

TEM was also used to examine the morphology of GOQDs. The GOQDs were monodispersed in an aqueous solution, as illustrated in Fig. S2A. Mean diameter of ~15 nm was found in the size distribution. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2B), the two peaks at 3432 and 2973 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of -OH and C-H. Similarly, peaks at 1000–2000 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of epoxy, C-OH/-COO of carboxylic groups, and the stretching vibration of CC of the aromatic ring, etc. [41,42]. The elemental and functional groups of GOQDs were studied using XPS. As shown in Fig. S2C, the spectrum displayed two primary peaks for carbon and oxygen at 284.3 eV (C 1 s) and 530.5 eV (O 1 s), respectively [43]. XPS spectrum of the C 1 s scan exhibited two peaks at 284.1 and 287.4 eV (Fig. S2D). The signal at 284.1 eV was more intense and could be attributed to the sp2 carbon atoms of the GOQDs structure, whereas the peak at 287.4 eV was associated with carboxyl groups [44]. The ζ-potential measurements of GOQDs exhibited a value of − 10.5 mV (Fig. S2E). Additionally, UV–vis absorption revealed a characteristic absorption peak at approximately 542 nm. The absorption band highly overlapped with the fluorescence emission peak of the fluorescent probes at 545 nm, indicating that FRET could occur between GOQDs (acceptors) and fluorescent probes (donors) (Fig. S3).

The nuc ssDNA played an important role in quenching the UCNPs by GOQDs. When GOQDs solution was mixed with the UCNPs-ssDNA as fluorescent probe, the upconversion fluorescence of the UCNPs-ssDNA gradually decreased first and then reached equilibrium as the amount of GOQDs increased (Fig. S4A). For fluorescence quenching, this was because the interactions of the GOQDs and ssDNA could bring GOQDs to the surface of the UCNPs-ssDNA and FRET occurred. Subsequently, a series of GOQDs at various concentrations were incubated with COOH-UCNPs without the ssDNA. The findings showed that there was almost no fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4B). The explanation for this should be because both the COOH-UCNPs and GOQDs are negatively charged, the repulsion between two nanomaterials prevented them from approaching each other and the FRET did not occur. It is evident from this result that nuc ssDNA plays a critical role in the interactions between fluorescent probes and GOQDs by allowing GOQDs to quench upconversion fluorescence generated by fluorescent probes.

To get the best performance of the developed genosensor for S. aureus detection, a series of related parameters were optimized. To begin, the amount of GOQDs (0–800 μL) was tested to attain a high fluorescence energy transfer efficiency. The fluorescence intensity at 545 nm was lowest when the amount of GOQDs was 600 μL, indicating that the ssDNA loaded on the UCNPs could interact with the maximum number of GOQDs, and the interactions could bring GOQDs to the surface of the UCNPs-ssDNA to achieve the highest energy transfer efficiency. Therefore, 600 μL was the best quantity of GOQDs ( Fig. 3A). Afterward, the incubation time of the detection system was measured. As seen in Fig. 3B, the intensity of the fluorescence decreased as the incubation period increased. The fluorescence intensity tended to stabilize as the time approached 1 h, which revealed that when ssDNA on the surface of UCNPs did not hybridize with the target nuc and only UCNPs-ssDNA and GOQDs were incubated in the system, the time was required for the fluorescence of UCNPs-ssDNA to be quenched to the maximum extent by GOQDs. Therefore, 1 h was chosen as the ideal incubation time. Furthermore, Fig. 3C showed the test result of incubation temperatures, which demonstrated that the quenching effectiveness was maximum at 40 °C. The cause might be due to beneficial hybridization of ssDNA on the surface of UCNPs and the target nuc to form the UCNPs-dsDNA, and improved interactions between the unhybridized ssDNA of the fluorescent probes and GOQDs at this temperature. As a result, the incubation temperature was set at 40 °C.

Under ideal experimental conditions, the genosensor was utilized to detect the standard target nuc gene in concentrations range from 1 × 10−17 to 1 × 10−11 mol L−1. The correlation between the fluorescence intensities and concentrations of S. aureus nuc was further observed. As demonstrated in Fig. 4A and B, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of target nuc concentration, which might explain the rise in dsDNA formation and separation of the GOQDs and fluorescent probes. Furthermore, an excellent linear relationship (F=414.3 Log Cnuc+10530, R2 = 0.9919) was obtained between the fluorescence signal intensities at 545 nm and the target nuc concentration in the range of 1 × 10−17-1 × 10−11 mol L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.98 × 10−17 mol L−1 by the formula 3 S/K, where S represents the standard deviation of 10 measurements of the blank sample without oligos (n = 10) and K indicates the slope of the standard curve [32].

Additionally, a comparative study was executed to know the analytical performances of the real-time PCR (Fig. S5) and numerous previously described approaches for S. aureus detection, and the results are shown in Table S2. When compared to these detection methodologies, the developed genosensor had a lower LOD, indicating that this analytic method was successful and could be used for sensitive detection of S. aureus nuc. The exceptional fluorescence properties of UCNPs and the remarkable quenching effect of GOQDs contributed to the sensor's increased sensitivity. Furthermore, the genosensor’s great specificity was due to the strong match stability of the proposed recognition probes to the target nuc ssDNA. These features, together with other key advantages, such as low cost and ease of application, suggested that the genosensor might be a promising practical option for detecting other pathogenic bacteria.

Under the same experimental settings, the efficiency of the proposed detection system towards complementary oligos and other numbers of base mismatch was studied to assess the specificity of this genosensor. The same concentration (1 ×10−13 mol L−1) was utilized in the experiments. The fluorescence intensity decreased as the number of base mismatches increased, as seen in Fig. 5A. The rise in mismatch number would result in a decrease in dsDNA stability, which would reduce dsDNA formation and fluorescence emission significantly.

This proved that the sensor has a high level of selectivity. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 545 nm for the target nuc was substantially greater than that for the oligos with base mismatches (Fig. 5B), confirming the selectivity of the fluorescent genosensor.

To verify whether the designed primers could specifically distinguish S. aureus, the contrast test was executed among B. cereus, S. typhimurium, and E. coli O157:H7. Both Fig. 6A and Fig. S6 revealed a single amplified target band, regardless of whether the target bacteria contained S. aureus (ATCC® 29213™) or S. aureus (ATCC® 6538™), demonstrating the specificity of the primers used for S. aureus. Although E. coli O157:H7 gDNA was used for the amplification and products were generated in lane 4 (Fig. 6A), the fluorescence intensity at 545 nm did not significantly change during the detection process (Fig. S7). This indicated that no target nuc in the products were complementary to recognition probes on the surface of the UCNPs. The products in lane 4 were non-target products and there was no nuc gene in E. coli O157:H7. In addition, milk with and without S. aureus was used for the gDNA extraction to amplify the nuc gene. The amplification products were only found in lane 4 (Fig. 6B), suggesting that no S. aureus in the original milk samples. The samples could be used for hybridization detection. For the assay, PCR amplification products were diluted to 0.45 × 10−13 mol L−1. The target nuc in prepared samples was detected by the designed fluorescent genosensor. Good recoveries ( Table 1) were obtained (95.67–101.69% for S. aureus ATCC® 29213™ and 95.40–102.19% for S. aureus ATCC® 6538™), demonstrating the accuracy of the genosensor and its potential for S. aureus detection in milk.

Furthermore, to estimate the reproducibility and feasibility in different samples, the developed method was used in milk and normal human serum spiked with S. aureus ATCC® 29213™. For the assay, PCR amplification products were diluted to 0.20 × 10−13 mol L−1 as the background concentration. The spiked samples had a concentration of 0.20 × 10−13, 0.40 × 10−13, 0.60 × 10−13 and 0.80 × 10−13 mol L−1, each of which was mixed with the diluted amplification products and analyzed as listed in Table 2. For eight days, reproducibility tests were carried out consecutively. Processing the relative standard deviations (RSD) of reproducibility was used to measure precision [45]. The results showed that the genosensor had high practicability and reliability for S. aureus detection, with the feasibility proven in milk and normal human serum.

Significantly, the developed method could be used for the detection of other pathogens in actual samples by replacing recognition probes and primers. For example, L. monocytogenes ATCC® 19115™ in milk was detected by the proposed method. The recognition probes and primers were designed and used according to L. monocytogenes-specific gene inlA and their sequences were exhibited in Table S1 . Recovery results were obtained and showed in Table S3, which indicated that this method could be used for the detection of the L. monocytogenes in milk by replacing recognition probes and specific primers.

To summarize, the outstanding sensitivity and wonderful accuracy of the developed fluorescent genosensor for S. aureus detection affirmed that the design strategy based on the fluorescent probes and GOQDs was effective and could be used for other pathogens detection by changing corresponding recognition probes and primers. According to what we know, this was a unique study in S. aureus nuc detection based on the integration of superior upconversion technology and molecular biology technology. This innovative design made use of the excellent fluorescence properties of UCNPs, specific recognition probes, and superior quenching capacity of GOQDs. The sensor has a high sensitivity for the detection of the S. aureus nuc gene, with a LOD of 0.98 × 10−17 mol L−1 and a linear range of 1 × 10−17 to 1 × 10−11 mol L−1. Furthermore, FRET was only activated when the target nuc completely matched with recognition probes on the UCNPs surface, demonstrating the sensor's great selectivity and potentially limiting interference with other pathogens. This fluorescent genosensor was successfully and effectively applied to real sample analysis, demonstrating that it has a significant potential for sensitive and accurate detection of harmful bacteria in food safety and environmental monitoring.

Rui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Suleiman A. Haruna: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shujat Ali: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Jing Xu: Investigation, Resources. Yunlian Zhang: Methodology. Lu Peng Lü: Resources, Validation. Huanhuan Li: Resources, Validation. Quansheng Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK2020379), Jiangsu Provincial Agricultural S&T Innovation Foundation (CX (20)2015), Postgraduate Research and Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX19_1630) and Project of Faculty of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131311.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rui Liu is a doctoral candidate in Jiangsu University.
Suleiman A. Haruna is a doctoral candidate in Jiangsu University.
Shujat Ali is a researcher in Wenzhou University.
Jing Xu is a doctoral candidate in Jiangsu University.
Yunlian Zhang is a master’s student in Jiangsu University.

Peng Lü is an associate researcher in Jiangsu University. His research focuses on the screening and application of genetically engineered antibody and the detection and application of SHERLOCK nucleic acid.
Huanhuan Li is an assistant professor in Jiangsu University. Her research focuses on the development of novel sensors.
Quansheng Chen is a professor in Jiangsu University. His research focuses on the development of novel fluorescence and SERS biosensor for harmful residues detection in foodstuff.
